principle: Vamana (Therapeutic emesis) purifies Kapha dosha from body

training_response: Vamana (Therapeutic emesis) is a Panchakarma procedure targeting Kapha dosha. Indications: Kapha diseases, respiratory disorders, skin diseases, obesity, poisoning, diabetes (Kapha type). Contraindications: Vata excess, emaciation, pregnancy, children, elderly, heart diseases. Pre-procedure: 3-7 days oleation (snehana) and sudation (swedana). Procedure: Administer emetic herbs (madanaphala, yastimadhu, rock salt) in milk or sugarcane juice; patient vomits 4-8 times. Post-procedure: Graduated diet: medicated smoke, rest, gradual return to normal diet over 7 days. Source: Su15, Si6.

name: Virechana (Therapeutic purgation)
therapeutic_uses: Pitta diseases, skin diseases, liver disorders, jaundice, fever, blood disorders
contraindications: Vata excess, diarrhea, rectal prolapse, emaciation, children, elderly
condition_disease: Pitta diseases, skin diseases, liver disorders, jaundice, fever, blood disorders
preparation_method: Administer purgative herbs (trivrit, haritaki, castor oil) in appropriate medium at appropriate time; patient purges 20-30 times
diet_recommendation: Graduated diet, rest, gradual return to normal diet
lifestyle_recommendation: Graduated diet, rest, gradual return to normal diet
principle: Virechana (Therapeutic purgation) purifies Pitta dosha from body

training_response: Virechana (Therapeutic purgation) is a Panchakarma procedure targeting Pitta dosha. Indications: Pitta diseases, skin diseases, liver disorders, jaundice, fever, blood disorders. Contraindications: Vata excess, diarrhea, rectal prolapse, emaciation, children, elderly. Pre-procedure: Oleation 3-7 days, then sudation. Procedure: Administer purgative herbs (trivrit, haritaki, castor oil) in appropriate medium at appropriate time; patient purges 20-30 times. Post-procedure: Graduated diet, rest, gradual return to normal diet. Source: Su15, Si6.

name: Basti (Therapeutic enema)
therapeutic_uses: Vata diseases, constipation, joint disorders, neurological disorders, post-delivery care
contraindications: Ama, fever, indigestion, bleeding from lower tract, diarrhea, Kapha diseases in early stage
condition_disease: Vata diseases, constipation, joint disorders, neurological disorders, post-delivery care
preparation_method: Unctuous enema (sneha basti): oil-based. Non-unctuous (niruha basti): decoction-based. Administer at appropriate time. Classic: 8 unctuous alternating with 1 non-unctuous
diet_recommendation: Rest lying on back for 30-45 min; graduated diet; specific post-enema regimen
lifestyle_recommendation: Rest lying on back for 30-45 min; graduated diet; specific post-enema regimen
principle: Basti (Therapeutic enema) purifies Vata dosha from body
training_prompt: What is Basti (Therapeutic enema) in Ayurveda according to Charaka Samhita? When is it used and how is it performed?
training_response: Basti (Therapeutic enema) is a Panchakarma procedure targeting Vata dosha. Indications: Vata diseases, constipation, joint disorders, neurological disorders, post-delivery care. Contraindications: Ama, fever, indigestion, bleeding from lower tract, diarrhea, Kapha diseases in early stage. Pre-procedure: Light oleation and sudation. Procedure: Unctuous enema (sneha basti): oil-based. Non-unctuous (niruha basti): decoction-based. Administer at appropriate time. Classic: 8 unctuous alternating with 1 non-unctuous. Post-procedure: Rest lying on back for 30-45 min; graduated diet; specific post-enema regimen. Source: Si8, Su19.

name: Nasya (Nasal therapy)
therapeutic_uses: Head diseases, sinusitis, migraine, ENT disorders, neurological conditions, eye diseases
contraindications: Pregnancy, menstruation, immediately after meals or bathing, fever
condition_disease: Head diseases, sinusitis, migraine, ENT disorders, neurological conditions, eye diseases
preparation_method: Instill 4-8 drops of medicated oil or decoction into nostrils; patient inhales deeply; spit out mucus; avoid cold and wind
diet_recommendation: Keep warm, avoid cold, take medicated smoking, rest for 30 min
lifestyle_recommendation: Keep warm, avoid cold, take medicated smoking, rest for 30 min
principle: Nasya (Nasal therapy) purifies Kapha/Vata in head dosha from body
training_prompt: What is Nasya (Nasal therapy) in Ayurveda according to Charaka Samhita? When is it used and how is it performed?
training_response: Nasya (Nasal therapy) is a Panchakarma procedure targeting Kapha/Vata in head dosha. Indications: Head diseases, sinusitis, migraine, ENT disorders, neurological conditions, eye diseases. Contraindications: Pregnancy, menstruation, immediately after meals or bathing, fever. Pre-procedure: Facial massage and steam to face. Procedure: Instill 4-8 drops of medicated oil or decoction into nostrils; patient inhales deeply; spit out mucus; avoid cold and wind. Post-procedure: Keep warm, avoid cold, take medicated smoking, rest for 30 min. Source: Su5, Su20.

name: Raktamokshana (Blood-letting)
therapeutic_uses: Skin diseases, inflammation, blood disorders, abscesses, non-healing wounds, gout
contraindications: Anemia, emaciation, children, elderly, pregnancy
condition_disease: Skin diseases, inflammation, blood disorders, abscesses, non-healing wounds, gout
preparation_method: Leech application (best method), venesection, or cupping. Leech therapy: soak in water, apply to clean area, remove when pale or full
diet_recommendation: Local antiseptic application, bandage, rest
lifestyle_recommendation: Local antiseptic application, bandage, rest
principle: Raktamokshana (Blood-letting) purifies Pitta and blood (rakta) dosha from body
training_prompt: What is Raktamokshana (Blood-letting) in Ayurveda according to Charaka Samhita? When is it used and how is it performed?
training_response: Raktamokshana (Blood-letting) is a Panchakarma procedure targeting Pitta and blood (rakta) dosha. Indications: Skin diseases, inflammation, blood disorders, abscesses, non-healing wounds, gout. Contraindications: Anemia, emaciation, children, elderly, pregnancy. Pre-procedure: Oleation. Procedure: Leech application (best method), venesection, or cupping. Leech therapy: soak in water, apply to clean area, remove when pale or full. Post-procedure: Local antiseptic application, bandage, rest. Source: Su14, Su24.

name: Snehana (Oleation/Oil therapy)
therapeutic_uses: Preparatory to main Panchakarma; Vata diseases; dryness; pre-procedure; emaciation
contraindications: Ama, indigestion, fever, Kapha excess, obesity
condition_disease: Preparatory to main Panchakarma; Vata diseases; dryness; pre-procedure; emaciation
preparation_method: Internal oleation: ghee or oil with food 3-7 days. External: Abhyanga (oil massage) with appropriate oil to whole body
diet_recommendation: Followed by Swedana
lifestyle_recommendation: Followed by Swedana
principle: Snehana (Oleation/Oil therapy) purifies Vata primarily dosha from body
training_prompt: What is Snehana (Oleation/Oil therapy) in Ayurveda according to Charaka Samhita? When is it used and how is it performed?
training_response: Snehana (Oleation/Oil therapy) is a Panchakarma procedure targeting Vata primarily dosha. Indications: Preparatory to main Panchakarma; Vata diseases; dryness; pre-procedure; emaciation. Contraindications: Ama, indigestion, fever, Kapha excess, obesity. Pre-procedure: None (this is pre-procedure itself). Procedure: Internal oleation: ghee or oil with food 3-7 days. External: Abhyanga (oil massage) with appropriate oil to whole body. Post-procedure: Followed by Swedana. Source: Su13, Si6.

name: Swedana (Sudation/Sweat therapy)
therapeutic_uses: Vata-Kapha diseases, joint stiffness, respiratory, preparatory to Panchakarma
contraindications: Pitta excess, bleeding disorders, pregnancy, emaciation, unconsciousness
condition_disease: Vata-Kapha diseases, joint stiffness, respiratory, preparatory to Panchakarma
preparation_method: Steam bath (baspa swedana), hot poultice (pinda sweda), kizhi (bolus massage), tub bath in medicated decoction
diet_recommendation: Followed by main Panchakarma procedure
lifestyle_recommendation: Followed by main Panchakarma procedure
principle: Swedana (Sudation/Sweat therapy) purifies Vata and Kapha dosha from body
training_prompt: What is Swedana (Sudation/Sweat therapy) in Ayurveda according to Charaka Samhita? When is it used and how is it performed?
training_response: Swedana (Sudation/Sweat therapy) is a Panchakarma procedure targeting Vata and Kapha dosha. Indications: Vata-Kapha diseases, joint stiffness, respiratory, preparatory to Panchakarma. Contraindications: Pitta excess, bleeding disorders, pregnancy, emaciation, unconsciousness. Pre-procedure: Oleation (Snehana) first. Procedure: Steam bath (baspa swedana), hot poultice (pinda sweda), kizhi (bolus massage), tub bath in medicated decoction. Post-procedure: Followed by main Panchakarma procedure. Source: Su14, Si6.

name: Panchamahabhuta theory
principle: All matter is composed of 5 elements: Akasha (space), Vayu (air), Tejas (fire), Jala (water), Prithvi (earth). Every substance and every body is composed of these. Doshas, dhatus, and malas arise from these 5 elements
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Panchamahabhuta theory as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Panchamahabhuta theory: All matter is composed of 5 elements: Akasha (space), Vayu (air), Tejas (fire), Jala (water), Prithvi (earth). Every substance and every body is composed of these. Doshas, dhatus, and malas arise from these 5 elements. Source: Su26.

name: Tridosha theory
principle: Vata (movement, air+space), Pitta (transformation, fire+water), Kapha (cohesion, water+earth) are the 3 biological energies governing all physiological and psychological processes. Health = balance of doshas; Disease = imbalance
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Tridosha theory as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Tridosha theory: Vata (movement, air+space), Pitta (transformation, fire+water), Kapha (cohesion, water+earth) are the 3 biological energies governing all physiological and psychological processes. Health = balance of doshas; Disease = imbalance. Source: Su1, Su18.

name: Sapta dhatu (7 tissues)
principle: Rasa (plasma) → Rakta (blood) → Mamsa (muscle) → Meda (fat) → Asthi (bone) → Majja (marrow) → Shukra (reproductive tissue). Each nourishes the next; disease in dhatus produces specific symptoms
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Sapta dhatu (7 tissues) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Sapta dhatu (7 tissues): Rasa (plasma) → Rakta (blood) → Mamsa (muscle) → Meda (fat) → Asthi (bone) → Majja (marrow) → Shukra (reproductive tissue). Each nourishes the next; disease in dhatus produces specific symptoms. Source: Su17, Su28.

name: Agni (Digestive fire)
principle: Jatharagni (main digestive fire in stomach/small intestine) is the key to health. 4 states: Sama (balanced), Vishama (irregular - V type), Tikshna (sharp - P type), Manda (slow - K type). All disease begins with impaired Agni
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Agni (Digestive fire) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Agni (Digestive fire): Jatharagni (main digestive fire in stomach/small intestine) is the key to health. 4 states: Sama (balanced), Vishama (irregular - V type), Tikshna (sharp - P type), Manda (slow - K type). All disease begins with impaired Agni. Source: Vi6, Su12.

name: Ama formation
principle: When Agni is impaired, undigested food metabolite Ama forms. Ama blocks channels (srotas), causes disease. Signs: coated tongue, bad breath, heaviness, fatigue after eating, undigested food in stool
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Ama formation as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Ama formation: When Agni is impaired, undigested food metabolite Ama forms. Ama blocks channels (srotas), causes disease. Signs: coated tongue, bad breath, heaviness, fatigue after eating, undigested food in stool. Source: Su28.

name: Ojas (Vital essence)
principle: Ojas is the essence of all 7 dhatus and the 3 doshas when in balance. It is the seat of immunity and life force. Located in heart. Two types: Para ojas (8 drops, essential for life) and Apara ojas (half anjali quantity)
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Ojas (Vital essence) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Ojas (Vital essence): Ojas is the essence of all 7 dhatus and the 3 doshas when in balance. It is the seat of immunity and life force. Located in heart. Two types: Para ojas (8 drops, essential for life) and Apara ojas (half anjali quantity). Source: Su17.

name: Srotas (channels)
principle: 16 channels in the body transport substances. If srotas are blocked (by Ama or vitiated doshas), disease occurs in those tissues. Treatment involves cleansing channels through Panchakarma
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Srotas (channels) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Srotas (channels): 16 channels in the body transport substances. If srotas are blocked (by Ama or vitiated doshas), disease occurs in those tissues. Treatment involves cleansing channels through Panchakarma. Source: Su11.

name: Cause of disease (Nidana)
principle: Three causes: Asatmyendriyartha Samyoga (improper use of sense organs), Prajna-paradha (intellectual errors/deliberate wrong actions), Parinama (time/seasonal effects). Most diseases arise from improper diet and conduct
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Cause of disease (Nidana) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Cause of disease (Nidana): Three causes: Asatmyendriyartha Samyoga (improper use of sense organs), Prajna-paradha (intellectual errors/deliberate wrong actions), Parinama (time/seasonal effects). Most diseases arise from improper diet and conduct. Source: Su1, Su11.

name: Ayu (Life) definition
principle: Ayu is the combination of Sharira (body), Indriya (senses), Sattva (mind), Atma (soul). The ideal life (Hitayu) is virtuous and beneficial to all. Long life (Dirghayu) is the goal of Ayurveda
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Ayu (Life) definition as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Ayu (Life) definition: Ayu is the combination of Sharira (body), Indriya (senses), Sattva (mind), Atma (soul). The ideal life (Hitayu) is virtuous and beneficial to all. Long life (Dirghayu) is the goal of Ayurveda. Source: Su1.

name: Purusha (Individual) constitution
principle: Each person has a unique Prakriti (constitution) determined at conception by dominant dosha(s). Vikruti is the current state of imbalance. Treatment aims to restore Vikruti to Prakriti
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Purusha (Individual) constitution as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Purusha (Individual) constitution: Each person has a unique Prakriti (constitution) determined at conception by dominant dosha(s). Vikruti is the current state of imbalance. Treatment aims to restore Vikruti to Prakriti. Source: Su8, Vi8.

name: Similarities and opposites principle (Samanya/Vishesha)
principle: Like increases like (Samanya) and opposites decrease each other (Vishesha). This is the basis of all Ayurvedic treatment: use opposite qualities to reduce aggravated doshas
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Similarities and opposites principle (Samanya/Vishesha) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Similarities and opposites principle (Samanya/Vishesha): Like increases like (Samanya) and opposites decrease each other (Vishesha). This is the basis of all Ayurvedic treatment: use opposite qualities to reduce aggravated doshas. Source: Su1.

name: Rasas (6 tastes)
principle: Sweet (Madhura), Sour (Amla), Salty (Lavana), Pungent (Katu), Bitter (Tikta), Astringent (Kashaya). Each taste has specific effects on doshas. Complete meals should include all 6 tastes. Taste → secondary taste (Anurasa) → post-digestive taste (Vipaka)
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Rasas (6 tastes) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Rasas (6 tastes): Sweet (Madhura), Sour (Amla), Salty (Lavana), Pungent (Katu), Bitter (Tikta), Astringent (Kashaya). Each taste has specific effects on doshas. Complete meals should include all 6 tastes. Taste → secondary taste (Anurasa) → post-digestive taste (Vipaka). Source: Su1, Su26.

name: Virya (potency/energy)
principle: Drugs and foods have 2 main potencies: Ushna (hot) and Sheeta (cold). Hot virya: increases Pitta, reduces Vata-Kapha; Cold virya: increases Vata-Kapha, reduces Pitta. Virya overrides taste in some situations
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Virya (potency/energy) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Virya (potency/energy): Drugs and foods have 2 main potencies: Ushna (hot) and Sheeta (cold). Hot virya: increases Pitta, reduces Vata-Kapha; Cold virya: increases Vata-Kapha, reduces Pitta. Virya overrides taste in some situations. Source: Su26.

name: Vipaka (post-digestive effect)
principle: Three vipakas: Madhura (sweet) - V-P pacifying; Amla (sour) - V-pacifying, P-K aggravating; Katu (pungent) - K-V pacifying, drying. Vipaka determines the long-term effect of a substance
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Vipaka (post-digestive effect) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Vipaka (post-digestive effect): Three vipakas: Madhura (sweet) - V-P pacifying; Amla (sour) - V-pacifying, P-K aggravating; Katu (pungent) - K-V pacifying, drying. Vipaka determines the long-term effect of a substance. Source: Su26.

name: Prabhava (special potency)
principle: Beyond taste, potency and vipaka, some substances have unique unexplainable actions called Prabhava. Example: gold reduces Pitta despite hot properties. Prabhava overrides all other actions
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Prabhava (special potency) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Prabhava (special potency): Beyond taste, potency and vipaka, some substances have unique unexplainable actions called Prabhava. Example: gold reduces Pitta despite hot properties. Prabhava overrides all other actions. Source: Su26.

name: Dravyaguna (pharmacology)
principle: Properties of substances: Rasa (taste), Guna (physical qualities), Virya (potency), Vipaka (post-digestive), Prabhava (special action), Karma (actions). Understanding these allows therapeutic use of any substance
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Dravyaguna (pharmacology) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Dravyaguna (pharmacology): Properties of substances: Rasa (taste), Guna (physical qualities), Virya (potency), Vipaka (post-digestive), Prabhava (special action), Karma (actions). Understanding these allows therapeutic use of any substance. Source: Su26.

name: Four pillars of treatment
principle: Physician, Drug, Attendant, Patient - all four must be of good quality for successful treatment. Physician is most important. Drug quality: locally available, effective, multiple forms, proper composition
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Four pillars of treatment as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Four pillars of treatment: Physician, Drug, Attendant, Patient - all four must be of good quality for successful treatment. Physician is most important. Drug quality: locally available, effective, multiple forms, proper composition. Source: Su9.

name: Rasayana (rejuvenation)
principle: Rasayana therapy promotes longevity, intelligence, strength, and prevents disease. Two types: Kutipraveshika (indoor, intensive) and Vatatapika (outdoor, simple). Key rasayanas: Chyavanprash, Amalaki, Ashwagandha, Shatavari, Guduchi
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Rasayana (rejuvenation) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Rasayana (rejuvenation): Rasayana therapy promotes longevity, intelligence, strength, and prevents disease. Two types: Kutipraveshika (indoor, intensive) and Vatatapika (outdoor, simple). Key rasayanas: Chyavanprash, Amalaki, Ashwagandha, Shatavari, Guduchi. Source: Ci1.

name: Vajikarana (reproductive therapy)
principle: Science of promoting reproductive health and vigor. Uses: milk, ghee, ashwagandha, shatavari, vidarikanda, kapikacchu (mucuna), gold preparations. Beneficial for those aged 16-70 years
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Vajikarana (reproductive therapy) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Vajikarana (reproductive therapy): Science of promoting reproductive health and vigor. Uses: milk, ghee, ashwagandha, shatavari, vidarikanda, kapikacchu (mucuna), gold preparations. Beneficial for those aged 16-70 years. Source: Ci2.

name: Sattvavajaya (psychotherapy)
principle: Control of mind through proper knowledge, restraint, memory, concentration, wisdom. Includes: company of virtuous persons, study of scriptures, yoga, meditation. Treats mental doshas: Rajas and Tamas
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic principle of Sattvavajaya (psychotherapy) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Sattvavajaya (psychotherapy): Control of mind through proper knowledge, restraint, memory, concentration, wisdom. Includes: company of virtuous persons, study of scriptures, yoga, meditation. Treats mental doshas: Rajas and Tamas. Source: Su1, Su11.

name: Trividha pariksha (3-fold examination)
preparation_method: Darshana (direct observation/inspection), Sparshana (palpation/touch), Prashna (questioning/inquiry). These 3 form the basis of Ayurvedic examination
principle: Darshana (direct observation/inspection), Sparshana (palpation/touch), Prashna (questioning/inquiry). These 3 form the basis of Ayurvedic examination
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic diagnostic method of Trividha pariksha (3-fold examination) according to Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Trividha pariksha (3-fold examination): Darshana (direct observation/inspection), Sparshana (palpation/touch), Prashna (questioning/inquiry). These 3 form the basis of Ayurvedic examination. Source: Vi4.

name: Ashtasthana pariksha (8-point examination)
preparation_method: Nadi (pulse), Mutra (urine), Mala (stool), Jihva (tongue), Shabda (speech/sounds), Sparsha (skin touch), Druk (eye examination), Akruti (overall appearance/build)
principle: Nadi (pulse), Mutra (urine), Mala (stool), Jihva (tongue), Shabda (speech/sounds), Sparsha (skin touch), Druk (eye examination), Akruti (overall appearance/build)

training_response: Ashtasthana pariksha (8-point examination): Nadi (pulse), Mutra (urine), Mala (stool), Jihva (tongue), Shabda (speech/sounds), Sparsha (skin touch), Druk (eye examination), Akruti (overall appearance/build). Source: Vi4.

name: Pulse diagnosis (Nadi pariksha)
preparation_method: Vata pulse: irregular, thin, moves like snake. Pitta pulse: jumping, like frog. Kapha pulse: slow, rolling, like swan. Examine at radial artery with 3 fingers. Best done at dawn before eating
principle: Vata pulse: irregular, thin, moves like snake. Pitta pulse: jumping, like frog. Kapha pulse: slow, rolling, like swan. Examine at radial artery with 3 fingers. Best done at dawn before eating
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic diagnostic method of Pulse diagnosis (Nadi pariksha) according to Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Pulse diagnosis (Nadi pariksha): Vata pulse: irregular, thin, moves like snake. Pitta pulse: jumping, like frog. Kapha pulse: slow, rolling, like swan. Examine at radial artery with 3 fingers. Best done at dawn before eating. Source: Vi4.

name: Tongue diagnosis (Jihva pariksha)
preparation_method: Healthy tongue: pink, moist, no coating. Vata: dry, rough, dark. Pitta: red, inflamed, yellow coat. Kapha: pale, wet, white thick coat. Ama: thick white coat at base
principle: Healthy tongue: pink, moist, no coating. Vata: dry, rough, dark. Pitta: red, inflamed, yellow coat. Kapha: pale, wet, white thick coat. Ama: thick white coat at base
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic diagnostic method of Tongue diagnosis (Jihva pariksha) according to Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Tongue diagnosis (Jihva pariksha): Healthy tongue: pink, moist, no coating. Vata: dry, rough, dark. Pitta: red, inflamed, yellow coat. Kapha: pale, wet, white thick coat. Ama: thick white coat at base. Source: Vi4.

name: Dosha diagnosis
preparation_method: Determine vitiated dosha by: symptoms matching dosha qualities, causative factors, time of day/season, body part affected, tissue layer involved. Then determine if increased or decreased, Sama (with Ama) or Nirama (without Ama)
principle: Determine vitiated dosha by: symptoms matching dosha qualities, causative factors, time of day/season, body part affected, tissue layer involved. Then determine if increased or decreased, Sama (with Ama) or Nirama (without Ama)
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic diagnostic method of Dosha diagnosis according to Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Dosha diagnosis: Determine vitiated dosha by: symptoms matching dosha qualities, causative factors, time of day/season, body part affected, tissue layer involved. Then determine if increased or decreased, Sama (with Ama) or Nirama (without Ama). Source: Vi6, Su18.

name: Prognosis assessment (Sadhya-Asadhya)
preparation_method: Curable (Sadhya): recent onset, single dosha, good patient strength. Difficult to cure (Yapya): chronic, multiple doshas, poor vitality. Incurable (Anupakrama): terminal signs present, multiple tissues involved, no ojas
principle: Curable (Sadhya): recent onset, single dosha, good patient strength. Difficult to cure (Yapya): chronic, multiple doshas, poor vitality. Incurable (Anupakrama): terminal signs present, multiple tissues involved, no ojas
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic diagnostic method of Prognosis assessment (Sadhya-Asadhya) according to Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Prognosis assessment (Sadhya-Asadhya): Curable (Sadhya): recent onset, single dosha, good patient strength. Difficult to cure (Yapya): chronic, multiple doshas, poor vitality. Incurable (Anupakrama): terminal signs present, multiple tissues involved, no ojas. Source: Su10, Ni1.

name: Patient strength assessment (Bala pariksha)
preparation_method: Examine: Prakruti (constitution), Vikruti (current state), Sara (tissue quality), Samhana (body compactness), Satmya (suitability), Sattva (mental strength), Aharashakti (digestive capacity), Vyayamashakti (exercise capacity), age
principle: Examine: Prakruti (constitution), Vikruti (current state), Sara (tissue quality), Samhana (body compactness), Satmya (suitability), Sattva (mental strength), Aharashakti (digestive capacity), Vyayamashakti (exercise capacity), age
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic diagnostic method of Patient strength assessment (Bala pariksha) according to Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Patient strength assessment (Bala pariksha): Examine: Prakruti (constitution), Vikruti (current state), Sara (tissue quality), Samhana (body compactness), Satmya (suitability), Sattva (mental strength), Aharashakti (digestive capacity), Vyayamashakti (exercise capacity), age. Source: Vi8.

name: Signs of death (Arishtam)
preparation_method: Multiple signs indicating imminent death: inverted/distorted body image, hair texture changes, sudden loss of sensation, specific pulse findings, particular combinations of symptoms. Physician should not treat these patients
principle: Multiple signs indicating imminent death: inverted/distorted body image, hair texture changes, sudden loss of sensation, specific pulse findings, particular combinations of symptoms. Physician should not treat these patients
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic diagnostic method of Signs of death (Arishtam) according to Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Signs of death (Arishtam): Multiple signs indicating imminent death: inverted/distorted body image, hair texture changes, sudden loss of sensation, specific pulse findings, particular combinations of symptoms. Excessive Vata in reproductive channels. Emotional and psychological factors. Improper timing (outside fertile period). Damage from wrong lifestyle during fetal development. Source: Sa4#30.

name: Vajikarana (Aphrodisiac/Reproductive therapy) - indications
therapeutic_uses: Vajikarana is for those who are: 16-70 years old, healthy, having desire for progeny, needing strength. Key substances: milk, ghee, ashwagandha, shatavari, vidarikanda, kapikacchu (mucuna), bala, atibala, sugar, honey, gold preparations, masturbation-free lifestyle

name: Postnatal care (Sutika paricharya)
therapeutic_uses: After delivery: light digestible foods initially; warm oil massage; vata-alleviating foods; ghee with pippali; warm sesame oil massage daily; avoid cold, heavy foods; warm water; avoid exertion; progressive diet from gruel to solid food over weeks; oleation and fomentation recommended

name: Determination of sex of fetus
therapeutic_uses: Male: elongated embryo in 2nd month. Female: round embryo. Neuter: tumor-like. Male: from paternal factors - valor, courage, skill, stability, heavy upper body. Female: from maternal factors - timidity, instability, heaviness in lower body, intolerance, tenderness. Balanced of both = neuter

name: Six therapeutic actions (Shad upakrama)
principle: 1) Langhana (reducing/fasting): lightens body; 2) Brimhana (nourishing/promoting): bulks body; 3) Rukshana (drying/roughening): reduces oiliness; 4) Snehana (oleation): adds lubrication; 5) Swedana (sudation/sweating): relieves stiffness, cold; 6) Stambhana (checking): restrains excessive movement. These 6 encompass all therapies

These 6 encompass all therapies. Source: Su22#3-8.

name: Four aims of Ayurveda (Chaturvidha purushartha)
principle: Ayurveda's aims: 1) Health maintenance for the healthy, 2) Treatment of diseases for the sick. Therapeutic approaches: 1) Shodhana (purification), 2) Shamana (pacification), 3) Ahara (dietary), 4) Vihara (lifestyle). All treatments fall under these categories
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic treatment principle of Four aims of Ayurveda (Chaturvidha purushartha) as described in Charaka Samhita.

Therapeutic approaches: 1) Shodhana (purification), 2) Shamana (pacification), 3) Ahara (dietary), 4) Vihara (lifestyle). All treatments fall under these categories. Source: Su1.

name: Sodhana vs. Shamana therapy
principle: Shodhana (purification/elimination): vomiting, purgation, enema, bloodletting, nasal therapy - for strong patients with high dosha accumulation. Shamana (pacification): digestives, carminatives, fasting, herbs - for moderate dosha vitiation, weak patients, or those who cannot undergo purification. Shodhana gives longer-lasting results; Shamana is gentler
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic treatment principle of Sodhana vs. Shamana therapy as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Sodhana vs. Shamana therapy: Shodhana (purification/elimination): vomiting, purgation, enema, bloodletting, nasal therapy - for strong patients with high dosha accumulation. Shamana (pacification): digestives, carminatives, fasting, herbs - for moderate dosha vitiation, weak patients, or those who cannot undergo purification. Shodhana gives longer-lasting results; Shamana is gentler. Source: Su16, Su22.

name: Pathya-apathya (wholesome-unwholesome food and behavior)
principle: Pathya (wholesome): what is suitable to channels and promotes health. Apathya (unwholesome): what blocks channels and causes disease. The physician must prescribe pathya diet and behavior specific to each disease. Without pathya, medicines alone cannot cure. With proper pathya, even without medicine, disease reduces
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic treatment principle of Pathya-apathya (wholesome-unwholesome food and behavior) as described in Charaka Samhita.

Apathya (unwholesome): what blocks channels and causes disease. The physician must prescribe pathya diet and behavior specific to each disease. Without pathya, medicines alone cannot cure. With proper pathya, even without medicine, disease reduces. Source: Su25.

name: Treatment of diseases with their opposite qualities
principle: Vata diseases: treat with sweet, sour, salty, unctuous, hot, heavy. Pitta diseases: treat with sweet, bitter, astringent, cold, heavy. Kapha diseases: treat with pungent, bitter, astringent, dry, hot, light. Always use qualities opposite to the vitiated dosha (Vishesha principle)
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic treatment principle of Treatment of diseases with their opposite qualities as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Treatment of diseases with their opposite qualities: Vata diseases: treat with sweet, sour, salty, unctuous, hot, heavy. Pitta diseases: treat with sweet, bitter, astringent, cold, heavy. Kapha diseases: treat with pungent, bitter, astringent, dry, hot, light. Always use qualities opposite to the vitiated dosha (Vishesha principle). Source: Su1, Vi6.

name: Proper sequence of treatment (Krama chikitsa)
principle: 1) Remove Ama first if present (langhana, digestives); 2) Preparatory procedures (snehana, swedana); 3) Main purification (shodhana - one of 5 Panchakarma); 4) Post-purificatory regimen (samsarjana krama - graduated diet); 5) Rejuvenation (rasayana) if appropriate. Skipping steps leads to complications
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic treatment principle of Proper sequence of treatment (Krama chikitsa) as described in Charaka Samhita.
training_response: Proper sequence of treatment (Krama chikitsa): 1) Remove Ama first if present (langhana, digestives); 2) Preparatory procedures (snehana, swedana); 3) Main purification (shodhana - one of 5 Panchakarma); 4) Post-purificatory regimen (samsarjana krama - graduated diet); 5) Rejuvenation (rasayana) if appropriate. Skipping steps leads to complications. Source: Su15, Si6.

name: Post-Panchakarma diet (Samsarjana krama)
principle: Graduated diet after major purification: Day 1-2: Peya (thin rice gruel). Day 3: Vilepi (thick gruel). Day 4: Akrita yusha (thin soup). Day 5: Krita yusha (seasoned soup). Day 6: Akrita mamsa (plain meat). Day 7: Krita mamsa (seasoned meat). Return to normal diet gradually over 7 days
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic treatment principle of Post-Panchakarma diet (Samsarjana krama) as described in Charaka Samhita.

Day 5: Krita yusha (seasoned soup). Day 6: Akrita mamsa (plain meat). Day 7: Krita mamsa (seasoned meat). Return to normal diet gradually over 7 days. Source: Si6.

name: Examination before treatment (Rogi pareeksha)
principle: Before treatment assess: 1) Prakruti (constitution), 2) Vikruti (morbidity), 3) Sara (tissue quality), 4) Samhana (compactness), 5) Satmya (suitability), 6) Sattva (mental strength), 7) Aharashakti (digestive capacity), 8) Vyayamashakti (exercise tolerance), 9) Age. Determine strength before prescribing potent drugs

Determine strength before prescribing potent drugs. Source: Vi8.

name: Drug administration rules (Aushadha sevana niyama)
principle: Timing matters: Before meals (pragbhakta): for lower body diseases, V. During meals (madhyabhakta): for digestive disorders. After meals (adho bhakta): for upper body, cough. With meals (sabhakta): for weakness. Between meals (antara bhakta): for frequent dosing. Early morning: for emesis. Night: for sleep, V-K. Never same time as incompatible substances
training_prompt: Explain the Ayurvedic treatment principle of Drug administration rules (Aushadha sevana niyama) as described in Charaka Samhita.

Never same time as incompatible substances. Source: Su1, Ci.

name: Upashaya (therapeutic trial)
principle: Upashaya is a diagnostic tool: administer diet, drug, or procedure and observe if condition improves (sapathya) or worsens (vipatna). If improves: diagnosis confirmed. If worsens: re-examine. Includes Dravya (substances), Rasa (taste), Guna (quality), Karma (action) upashayas. Highly useful when diagnosis is uncertain

If improves: diagnosis confirmed. If worsens: re-examine. Includes Dravya (substances), Rasa (taste), Guna (quality), Karma (action) upashayas. Highly useful when diagnosis is uncertain. Source: Ni1.

name: Incurable diseases (Asadhya vyadi)
principle: Do not treat: conditions with multiple terminal signs, long-standing multi-tissue involvement, diseases in vital locations with exhausted ojas, conditions where all 4 pillars of treatment are defective. The physician who attempts to treat the untreatable loses reputation. Identify such cases early and decline treatment

The physician who attempts to treat the untreatable loses reputation. Identify such cases early and decline treatment. Source: Su10, Ni8.

name: Importance of Agni in all treatments
principle: Agni (digestive fire) is the pivotal factor in health and disease. All Panchakarma, all herbs, all diet works through Agni. Protect and stimulate Agni in all treatments. Agni impairment (Agnimandya) underlies all diseases. Before treating any disease, assess and correct Agni. Never give medicines to one with Ama (sign of Agni impairment)

name: Shamidhanya (Legumes)
therapeutic_uses: Mung bean (mudga): best legume - light, easily digestible, V-P-K trisama, good for sick. Black gram (masha): heavy, nourishing, increases K, best for debility. Horse gram (kulatha): rough, hot, good for respiratory and kidney. Lentil (masura): light, astringent, good for eye. Green pea (kalaya): rough, heavy, K-aggravating. Chickpea (chanaka): light, astringent, K-reducing

name: Shaka (Vegetables)
therapeutic_uses: Bitter vegetables (patola, nimba, etc.): alleviate K and P, light, digestive. Leafy vegetables: generally light but some K-aggravating. Root vegetables: heavier, more nourishing. Mushrooms (except serpent mushroom): cold, heavy, K-aggravating. Best vegetables: amalaki (amla), patola, jivanti, shatavari leaves, moringa

name: Phala (Fruits)
therapeutic_uses: Best fruits: grapes (alleviates V-P, rasayana), pomegranate (tridosha samaka), amalaki (best rasayana). Mango: K-P aggravating when ripe. Dates: nourishing, V-P pacifying. Coconut: heavy, K-aggravating. Bilva: best digestive fruit. Unripe fruits: V-aggravating, astringent. Ripe fruits: generally sweeter, easier to digest

name: Kritanna (Prepared foods)
therapeutic_uses: Peya (thin gruel): most digestible, 1 part rice: 14 parts water. Vilepi (thick gruel): 1:4 ratio. Odana (cooked rice): 1:6 ratio. Manda (rice water): most easily digestible. Yavagu (barley gruel): excellent for fever. Khichdi (rice-lentil): more nourishing. Bread (apupa): heavier, more difficult. Processing affects digestibility significantly

therapeutic_uses: Carnivorous animals: best for wasting, TB, piles, grahani (meat soup). Wild game: better than domestic. Forest animals (deer, rabbit): light, V-P pacifying. Domestic animals (cow, goat): moderately heavy. Aquatic animals (fish): heavy, K-aggravating. Birds (peacock, partridge): light digestible. Pork: heavy, K-aggravating. Beef: strengthening

name: Krita taila (Processed oils)
therapeutic_uses: Sesame oil: best for external use, V-pacifying, hot. Castor oil: best purgative, V-pacifying internally. Mustard oil: stimulating, penetrating, skin diseases. Coconut oil: cooling, P-K disorders. Neem oil: antibacterial, skin diseases. Mahua oil: nourishing. Oil processing method (bhavana, murchhana) significantly alters oil properties

name: Gorasa (Dairy products)
therapeutic_uses: Fresh milk: heavy, best just-drawn from healthy cow. Cream: heaviest dairy. Curd (dadhi): hot, sour, K-aggravating; avoid at night; excellent for anemia. Buttermilk (takra): lightest dairy, best for grahani/IBS. Whey (mastu): light, digestive. Ghee (ghrita): best fat, light when used in small amounts. Butter: cold, K-aggravating

name: Jala (Water types)
therapeutic_uses: Rainwater (divya): purest, lightest, best for health. River water (nadija): depends on source and season. Well water (kupa): heavier. Pond water (tadaga): heaviest. Boiled water: lighter than fresh; add ginger or herbs for therapeutic use. Hot water: alleviates K and V, promotes digestion. Cold water: alleviates P and burning sensation

name: Madya (Wines/fermented beverages)
therapeutic_uses: Old wine: best quality - alleviates K, digestive. New wine: K-aggravating, less beneficial. Grain wines (sura): from rice, barley. Fruit wines (asava): from grapes, sugarcane. Herbal wines (arista): medicinal - Dashamoolarista, Ashwagandharishta, Abhayarista etc. All wines contraindicated in Pitta conditions, bleeding disorders, pregnancy

name: Asatmyendriyartha samyoga (Improper sensory use)
principle: Over-use of senses: staring at very bright light, listening to very loud sounds, excessive sexual activity, eating excessively. Under-use: avoiding use of senses. Wrong use: using senses for inappropriate objects. This is one of three fundamental causes of all disease
training_prompt: What does Charaka Samhita say about Asatmyendriyartha samyoga (Improper sensory use) as a cause of disease?
training_response: Asatmyendriyartha samyoga (Improper sensory use): Over-use of senses: staring at very bright light, listening to very loud sounds, excessive sexual activity, eating excessively. Under-use: avoiding use of senses. Wrong use: using senses for inappropriate objects. This is one of three fundamental causes of all disease. Source: Su1, Su11.

name: Prajnaparadha (Intellectual transgression)
principle: Deliberate wrong actions knowing them to be wrong; disregarding wisdom of scripture and elders; suppressing good impulses; acting under anger, greed, delusion, fear; ignoring proper time, place and manner. This is the most fundamental cause of all disease - the root of all disease in the mind
training_prompt: What does Charaka Samhita say about Prajnaparadha (Intellectual transgression) as a cause of disease?
training_response: Prajnaparadha (Intellectual transgression): Deliberate wrong actions knowing them to be wrong; disregarding wisdom of scripture and elders; suppressing good impulses; acting under anger, greed, delusion, fear; ignoring proper time, place and manner. This is the most fundamental cause of all disease - the root of all disease in the mind. Source: Su1, Sa1.

name: Parinama (Effect of time/seasonal changes)
principle: Natural changes of time, age and season that affect the body: children susceptible to Kapha, middle-aged to Pitta, elderly to Vata. Seasonal variations accumulate doshas. Untimely seasonal changes cause disease. Normal aging is natural parinama and leads to Vata-dominant old age
training_prompt: What does Charaka Samhita say about Parinama (Effect of time/seasonal changes) as a cause of disease?
training_response: Parinama (Effect of time/seasonal changes): Natural changes of time, age and season that affect the body: children susceptible to Kapha, middle-aged to Pitta, elderly to Vata. Seasonal variations accumulate doshas. Untimely seasonal changes cause disease. Normal aging is natural parinama and leads to Vata-dominant old age. Source: Su1, Su11.

name: Ama (Undigested food metabolite)
principle: Formed when Agni is impaired: undigested, sticky, foul-smelling, blocks channels. Acts as root of most chronic diseases. Precedes all pathological processes. Signs: coated tongue, morning fatigue, lack of appetite, feeling of heaviness after eating, mental fog, body odor. Treatment: Agni-kindling herbs and fasting
training_prompt: What does Charaka Samhita say about Ama (Undigested food metabolite) as a cause of disease?
training_response: Ama (Undigested food metabolite): Formed when Agni is impaired: undigested, sticky, foul-smelling, blocks channels. Acts as root of most chronic diseases. Precedes all pathological processes. Signs: coated tongue, morning fatigue, lack of appetite, feeling of heaviness after eating, mental fog, body odor. Treatment: Agni-kindling herbs and fasting. Source: Su28, Vi2.

name: Viharajanya (Lifestyle-related causes)
principle: Suppression of natural urges (major cause of Vata diseases); overexertion; inadequate exercise; improper postures; inappropriate sleep habits (daytime sleep, insufficient sleep); contaminated environment; overindulgence in pleasure; sexual excess
training_prompt: What does Charaka Samhita say about Viharajanya (Lifestyle-related causes) as a cause of disease?
training_response: Viharajanya (Lifestyle-related causes): Suppression of natural urges (major cause of Vata diseases); overexertion; inadequate exercise; improper postures; inappropriate sleep habits (daytime sleep, insufficient sleep); contaminated environment; overindulgence in pleasure; sexual excess. Source: Su7, Su11.

name: Aharajanya (Diet-related causes)
principle: Antagonistic foods; foods contrary to constitution or season; excessive or deficient eating; improper timing of meals; eating before previous meal digests; eating when not hungry; eating excessively heavy, cold, dry, raw or stale food; repeatedly heating food
training_prompt: What does Charaka Samhita say about Aharajanya (Diet-related causes) as a cause of disease?
training_response: Aharajanya (Diet-related causes): Antagonistic foods; foods contrary to constitution or season; excessive or deficient eating; improper timing of meals; eating before previous meal digests; eating when not hungry; eating excessively heavy, cold, dry, raw or stale food; repeatedly heating food. Source: Su26, Su28.

name: Manasika (Mental/psychological causes)
principle: Excessive grief (shoka), anger (krodha), fear (bhaya), lust (kama), greed (lobha), attachment (moha), envy (irshya), pride (mada) - these mental derangements directly cause both mental and physical diseases through mind-body connection. Mental causes often precede and perpetuate physical disease
training_prompt: What does Charaka Samhita say about Manasika (Mental/psychological causes) as a cause of disease?
training_response: Manasika (Mental/psychological causes): Excessive grief (shoka), anger (krodha), fear (bhaya), lust (kama), greed (lobha), attachment (moha), envy (irshya), pride (mada) - these mental derangements directly cause both mental and physical diseases through mind-body connection. Mental causes often precede and perpetuate physical disease. Source: Sa1, Su11.

name: Daivaja (Exogenous/karmic causes)
principle: Diseases arising from exogenous factors: infection, toxins, spirits, seasonal disasters, past karmic actions. These require divine therapy (mantras, gems, auspicious acts) in addition to rational therapy. Some exogenous diseases are beyond rational treatment. Epidemics (janpadoddhwamsa) arise from vitiation of air, water, land and season simultaneously
training_prompt: What does Charaka Samhita say about Daivaja (Exogenous/karmic causes) as a cause of disease?
training_response: Daivaja (Exogenous/karmic causes): Diseases arising from exogenous factors: infection, toxins, spirits, seasonal disasters, past karmic actions. Epidemics (janpadoddhwamsa) arise from vitiation of air, water, land and season simultaneously. Source: Vi3.

Tikshna Agni (Pitta): hyperacidity, quick digestion, irritability when hungry. Manda Agni (Kapha): slow digestion, heaviness after eating, excessive sleep, obesity

name: Pradhan (cardinal/main) symptoms of diseases
symptom: Each disease has specific cardinal symptoms (rupa): Jwara = fever. Atisara = loose stools. Prameha = turbid urine. Kustha = skin discoloration. Kasa = cough. Shwasa = dyspnoea. Hridroga = chest pain and palpitations. Grahani = alternating bowel habits. Identify disease by these cardinal symptoms before proceeding to treatment

name: Kutipraveshika rasayana (Indoor rejuvenation)
therapeutic_uses: Most potent form of Rasayana. Patient lives in specially built cottage (kuti) isolated from sun, wind and cold for 1-3 months. Complete rest and purification, then takes rasayana medicines in progressive doses. Results: longevity, freedom from disease, supreme intelligence, supreme strength, youthful appearance, all senses perfect. Only for those with proper resources

name: Vatatapika rasayana (Outdoor rejuvenation)
therapeutic_uses: Less intensive but practical form. Patient lives at home but follows strict regimen: purification first, then daily rasayana medicines (Chyavanprash, amalaki, ashwagandha etc.) with milk, ghee. No isolation required. Results are less dramatic but still significant. Suitable for most people

name: Amalaki rasayana (Indian gooseberry therapy)
therapeutic_uses: Amalaki (Emblica officinalis) is the best single rasayana. Method: take 1-5g fresh amalaki powder or juice with honey and ghee daily for months to years. Or prepare jam with ghee and honey. Benefits: longevity, prevents premature aging, improves all sense organs, intelligence, strength, complexion, voice; prevents hair loss, premature greying; anti-oxidant

name: Brahma Rasayana (Supreme rejuvenation formula)
therapeutic_uses: Main formula for maximum rasayana effect. Ingredients include: amalaki, ashwagandha, shatavari, pippali, haritaki, vibhitaki, brahmi, shatapushpa, vidari and many others cooked in ghee and honey. Take 10g with milk daily for extended period. Produces strength, vigor, nourishment

name: Silajatu (Shilajit) rasayana

When impaired causes: Infertility, sexual disorders, impotence, no semen, premature ejaculation, emotional instability. Nourishing foods: Milk, ghee, ashwagandha, shatavari, kapikacchu (mucuna), vidarikanda, safe aphrodisiacs. Source: Su17, Ci2.

name: Three mental doshas (Manasa doshas)
principle: Rajas (momentum, desire, action) and Tamas (inertia, dullness, darkness) are the two main mental doshas. Sattva (peaceful, clear, balanced, with knowledge) is the balanced state. Rajas and Tamas in excess cause mental disease. Treatment: Sattvavajaya - control through knowledge, restraint, memory and concentration

Treatment: Sattvavajaya - control through knowledge, restraint, memory and concentration. Source: Su1#57-58.

name: Sattvavajaya (Psychotherapy)
principle: The Ayurvedic system of psychotherapy: restraint (samyama), memory (smriti), concentration (dharana), reasoning (yukti), knowledge (jnana). Practical methods: company of virtuous persons (satpurusha seva), study of scriptures, yoga and meditation, righteous action, devotion, gratitude, service to teachers. This controls mental doshas Rajas and Tamas

name: Classification of mental constitution (Manas prakriti)
principle: Sattvika (pure): virtuous, compassionate, truthful, devoted, clean, knowledge-oriented - 16 types including Brahma, Mahendra etc. Rajasika: ambitious, energetic, proud, envious, active - 6 types. Tamasika: dull, sleepy, addicted, greedy, fearful, ignorant - 3 types. Mental constitution is determined at conception and affects mental health tendencies

Tamasika: dull, sleepy, addicted, greedy, fearful, ignorant - 3 types. Mental constitution is determined at conception and affects mental health tendencies. Source: Sa4, Vi8.

name: Mind-body relationship (Manas-sharira sambandha)
principle: Mental diseases cause physical diseases and vice versa. Passion (raga) associates with fever; grief (shoka) affects all doshas; fear (bhaya) aggravates Vata; anger (krodha) aggravates Pitta; attachment (moha) aggravates Kapha. Mind and body are inseparable in Ayurveda. Treating one without the other is incomplete

name: Manasika diseases (Mental disorders classification)
principle: Unmada (psychosis): incoherent speech, abnormal behavior. Apasmara (epilepsy): sudden loss of consciousness. Mada (intoxication). Murcha (fainting). Bhaya (pathological fear/anxiety). Shoka (grief disorder). Unmada types: Vataja (rapid speech, crying, fear), Pittaja (anger, violence), Kaphaja (silence, saliva), Sannipataja (all symptoms)

name: Role of Sattva (Mental strength) in healing
principle: Sattva (mental strength/will power) is a key factor in recovery. Strong Sattva: tolerates pain, complies with treatment, maintains diet. Weak Sattva: cannot tolerate procedures, poor compliance, worsens disease. Physician must assess Sattva before prescribing strong therapies. Practices to strengthen Sattva: yoga, devotion, study, virtuous company

name: What are the signs that a medicine or treatment is working properly according to...
principle: According to Charaka Samhita, signs of proper treatment include: reduction or disappearance of disease symptoms, restoration of normal Agni (digestive fire), regular and proper elimination of urine and feces, lightness in the body, cleansing of channels, appearance of normal appetite and thirst, restoration of strength and complexion, mental clarity and emotional balance, and overall sense of well-being. The physician monitors these signs to assess treatment efficacy. Source: Su22, Ci.

name: How does Charaka Samhita define health (Swastha)?...
principle: Charaka Samhita defines health (Swastha or Arogya) as the equilibrium of Doshas (Vata, Pitta, Kapha), Agni (digestive fire), Dhatus (7 tissues), and Malas (waste products), combined with a pleasant (prasanna) state of Atma (soul), Indriya (senses), and Manas (mind). 'Health is happiness; disease is unhappiness.' A healthy person has: balanced doshas, strong Agni, quality tissues, proper elimination, and psychological well-being. Source: Su1, Ci.

name: What is the Ayurvedic approach to preventive healthcare?...
principle: Charaka Samhita's approach to preventive healthcare includes: 1) Dinacharya (daily routine) - oil massage, exercise, proper sleep, oral hygiene, nasal oiling; 2) Ritucharya (seasonal regimen) - adapting diet and lifestyle to season; 3) Sadvritta (code of conduct) - ethical and behavioral guidelines; 4) Rasayana (rejuvenation) - periodic use of rejuvenating herbs; 5) Purification (Shodhana) - seasonal Panchakarma to eliminate accumulated doshas; 6) Sattvavajaya (mental hygiene) - yoga, meditation, virtuous company. The text states that 'one who follows the code of good conduct lives 100 years without disease.' Source: Su5, Su6, Su8.

name: What foods does Charaka Samhita say are universally beneficial?...
principle: Charaka Samhita identifies these as universally beneficial (pathya for all): old rice (shali, 1+ year), wheat, mung bean (mudga), rock salt (saindhava), amalaki (Indian gooseberry), ghee (in small amounts), honey (unheated), pure rainwater, and cow's milk. These are considered suitable for most constitutions and conditions. The text specifically warns that these 'best of each category' form the ideal diet. Source: Su25, Su27.

name: How should medicines be taken (before, during or after meals) according to Chara...
principle: Charaka Samhita provides specific rules for medicine timing: Pragbhakta (before meals): for lower body diseases, Vata in lower half. Madhyabhakta (during meals): for digestive disorders. Adhobhakta (after meals): for upper body diseases, cough. Sabhakta (with meals): for weakness, when medicine must be taken with food. Antara bhakta (between meals): for frequent dosing throughout day. Samudga (in 2 divided doses before and after): for hiccough, dyspnoea. Morning: for emesis medications. Night: for sleep disorders, Vata-Kapha in head. Source: Su1, Ci.

name: What does Charaka Samhita say about the ideal characteristics of a good physicia...
principle: Charaka Samhita describes the ideal physician (Vaidya) as having: excellence of theoretical knowledge (Shruta), practical experience (Drista), dexterity (Daksha), and cleanliness (Shucha). They should be: of high descent, well-versed in scripture, clean, skillful, self-controlled, expert in procedure. The four qualifications for success are: learning, wisdom, practical knowledge, and experience. The physician should have friendliness toward all, compassion for the diseased, attachment to helping the curable, and equanimity about those moving toward death. Source: Su9, Su29.

name: What is the Ayurvedic understanding of epidemic diseases (Janapadoddhwamsa)?...
principle: Charaka Samhita describes epidemics (Janapadoddhwamsa - destruction of communities) as arising from simultaneous vitiation of four essential environmental factors: Vayu (air), Jala (water), Desha (land/place), and Kala (time/season). When these become unwholesome, diseases spread through entire populations. Unlike individual diseases, epidemics cannot be attributed to personal diet or lifestyle alone. Prevention involves purification of environment, protective herbs (rasayanas), mantra, gems, virtuous conduct, and social harmony. Source: Vi3.

name: How does Charaka Samhita approach treatment of the elderly?...
principle: Charaka Samhita notes that old age (Jaravyadhi) is predominantly Vata-dominant. The elderly require: gentle treatments (no strong purgatives/emetics), nourishing (brimhana) therapies, oleation (snehana), mild enemas (matra basti - small dose enemas), warm oil massages (abhyanga), light easily-digestible foods, milk, ghee, rasayana medicines (Chyavanprash, ashwagandha), adequate rest, gentle exercise only. Strong purification is generally contraindicated. Rasayana therapy is especially beneficial for the elderly to reverse or slow aging. Source: Su22, Ci1.

name: What does Charaka Samhita say about the importance of sleep (Nidra)?...
principle: Charaka Samhita calls sleep (Nidra) one of the three pillars of life along with food (ahara) and celibacy (brahmacharya). Proper sleep: nourishes body, promotes growth (dhatu brimhana), restores strength, promotes complexion, knowledge and life. Disturbed sleep causes misery, weakness, impotence, ignorance and even death. Ideal sleep: 7-8 hours at night, head facing east or south, left side position. Daytime sleep (appropriate only in summer and for specific conditions) causes Kapha aggravation. Chronic insomnia is a Vata disorder. Source: Su21, Su11.

name: What is the Ayurvedic view on fasting (Upavasa)?...
principle: Charaka Samhita treats fasting (Upavasa/Langhana) as a primary therapeutic tool. Benefits: kindles Agni, reduces Ama, lightens body, purifies channels. Indications: Kapha diseases, obesity, ama-related conditions, early fever. Types: complete fast (water only), partial fast (gruel/peya only), fruit fast. Duration: until signs of proper langhana appear (lightness, hunger, clarity). Contraindications: Vata excess, emaciation, pregnancy, severe weakness, old age, children, anxiety disorders. Without proper Agni, fasting can aggravate Vata. Source: Su22, Ci1.

name: How does Charaka Samhita describe the relationship between diet and the mind?...
principle: Charaka Samhita establishes a direct mind-diet connection through the concept of 'Ahara becomes Manas' (food becomes mind). Sattvic foods (fresh, sweet, light, natural): promote clarity, intelligence, contentment, spiritual growth. Rajasic foods (spicy, bitter, salty, very hot): promote passion, aggression, restlessness. Tamasic foods (stale, heavy, fermented, meat, intoxicants): promote dullness, laziness, ignorance, sleep. Mental disorders can be treated partly through dietary modification. Conversely, mental states affect digestion and the ability to transform food into healthy tissues. Source: Sa4, Su27.
